title: Train Glossary Entry
author: Anonymous
variants:
THE PRY-PAUSE:
-
The half-second
before a chest opens.
The breath you hold
without meaning to.


A long pry-pause is
the kind we speak of —
the kind of a chest
worth waiting on.
Of shorter ones, nothing.

THE LONG LEAN:
-
What is said to happen
on a curve —
the whole carriage
tilts as one.


Those who have felt one
speak of it unprompted.
The rest of us
trust the telling.

A REBOARDER:
-
A passenger who claims
they got off the train
and came back.


Most are lying.
The ones who aren't
won't bring it up.
You learn to listen
for the silence.

RETURNED AROUND:
-
The next carriage
feels like one
you'd already crossed.
The certainty the train
must have turned.


No-one sees it happen.
You feel it after.
You ask the next
passenger if they did.
They never have.

WOBBLY TIME:
-
Time that won't
hold still long enough
to be measured.
The only kind here.


We try to count it.
Two passengers
comparing notes
will never agree
on how long.

A WARNING-DIM:
-
When the lanterns
dim twice in a row.
No-one decided this
meant something —
it just does.


You'll see passengers
quicken their step
without speaking.
Nothing makes them.
Most of us do anyway.

A SILENT FRIEND:
-
A passenger who greets
you with a nod
and nothing more.
Never unkind.
Just wordless.


Over enough carriages
you come to tell
one nod from another.
That is friendship,
of a kind.

A BIOME-SLIP:
-
When the world outside
changes. Desert to
forest. Forest to ice.
No-one quite sees
it happen.


The slip happens
in the looking-away.
The world waits
for you to turn
before it changes.

TO SETTLE-IN:
-
To stay in a carriage
long enough
that you stop counting
how long.


You can tell who has.
They have stopped
asking how long.
The question
no longer fits.

THE UNSTALE:
-
Bread you have carried
for so long
that it should be stale.
It isn't.
It never is.


Some refuse to eat it
once they notice.
The rest of us
eat it anyway.

THE DENAMED:
-
A passenger who can
no longer recall
their own name.
The carriages take it
quietly, over time.


You can sit beside one
and not know.
They have no name
to give you.

PASSING-WORDS:
-
What you say
to a passenger
you'll never see again.
They don't have
to mean anything.


A nod will do.
A word about weather.
The point is the saying,
not the said.

AN OLD-HOLD:
-
An item you have kept
so long
you no longer remember
bringing it
onto the train.


You forget when
it was new to you.
You forget
it was ever
not yours.

THE THRESHOLD-HOLD:
-
The half-second pause
before you step
from one carriage
into the next.


We do it without
deciding.
Something in us
checks the next
carriage is still there.

OFFLOADING:
-
The slow ritual
of sitting with
your things
and deciding
what to drop.


You always know
which thing to drop.
Sorting just lets
the hands catch up.

A SHARE-OUT:
-
An item you set down
in a chest, on purpose,
for the next person
who opens it.


You leave them
for whoever opens
the chest next.
You don't know who.
You hope.

A CARRY-DOWN:
-
A thing left in a chest
on purpose
by whoever opened it
before you.
Now yours.


You won't meet them.
You won't know who.
You carry the thing
forward, and maybe
leave one back.

TWO HANDS BOTH FULL:
-
The moment you find
two things you want,
and only one
will leave with you.


The lesser-wanted
becomes a share-out.
You feel both
choices in your hands
for a while after.

A POCKET-DARLING:
-
An item you keep
for its name,
its trim, its words.
You will never use it.
You will never drop it.


Everyone has one.
Most of us
have several.
We rarely admit
how many.

FIRST-GLINT:
-
The first half-second
of seeing something
in a chest
that you have not seen
before.


Before you know
what it is.
Before your hand
reaches.
A small light
behind the eyes.

A GOLDEN-STRIKE:
-
The moment you know
the item is one
you have never owned.
The first time
in the holding.


You don't shout.
You don't speak.
You sit with it.
The whole carriage
feels brighter than
just before.

A LOOK-PAST:
-
An item whose name
you decide not
to read twice.
The kind nobody
should have written.


You look past the name.
You set the item back.
Later, you try not to
recall what it said.
You pray to forget.

A QUICK-SHELVE:
-
An item whose
inscription reads
like a warning
not meant for you.


You shelve it fast.
You don't finish
the line.
You move on.
You don't look back.

AN INWARD-WHY:
-
A question you ask
yourself
that you have never
asked yourself
before.


Always the same shape.
Never the same answer.
Each carriage asks them
a little louder.

THE WAKING-DOUBT:
-
The question
that arrives
some carriages in.
Is any of this
actually here?


You touch the wall.
The wall is there.
This does not settle
the question.

A QUESTIONEER:
-
A passenger who has
stopped finding answers
and started finding
questions everywhere.


The chair, a question.
The lantern, the same.
You become a question
they ask without
opening their mouth.

PURE OF HEART:
-
A passenger said to
have crossed a thousand
carriages without
raising a hand.


Most doubt the count.
Few doubt the passenger.
You can tell one
by how still
their hands stay.
